name: Replanted Fruit Trees and Orchards, Appomattox Court House National Historical Park
note: Appomattox Court House National Historical Park is a rehabilitated 19th century Virginia courthouse town and surrounding agricultural landscape that commemorates Robert E. Lee’s surrender to Ulysses S. Grant there in 1865, ending the Civil War. The village homes, outbuildings, and businesses formed a hub for county government dating from the 1840s. Kitchen gardens, small vineyards, and orchards of fruit trees were...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.
parkName: Appomattox Court House National Historical Park

name: Apple Tree near Humpback Gap, Blue Ridge Parkway
note: The cultural landscape of the Humpback Rocks developed area, as part of the Blue Ridge Parkway, is a designed historic landscape that is significant in the areas of engineering, entertainment/recreation, landscape architecture, and transportation. While local farm fields and orchards preceding the establishment of the Blue Ridge Parkway are no longer in existence, vegetation remains within the Humpback Gap and Tra...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Pecan Orchard, Colonial National Historical Park
note: Jamestown is the site of the first permanent English settlement in North America, beginning in 1607. The settlement grew into a town and Virginia’s first capitol, and then after a period of abandonment, developed into land managed as plantations. The Revolutionary and Civil Wars also had an impact on the island through the construction of various fortifications and earthworks. Beginning in 1613, Governor Gates pla...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Orchard, Eisenhower National Historic Site
note: Eisenhower National Historic Site outside of Gettysburg, Pennsylvania preserves the home and farm of General Dwight D. Eisenhower, the thirty-fourth President of the United States. The areas now occupied by Farms #1, #2, #3 and Clement Redding Farm are believed to have been settled by the mid-eighteenth century with patents dating from 1749. In 1951, the Eisenhowers purchased what is now called Farm #1 from Allen...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Remnant Orchard, Fredericksburg & Spotsylvania National Military Park
note: Chatham Manor has a long and rich association with fruit trees. Among the earliest references to Chatham’s trees are from 1798 and 1799, when William Fitzhugh sent George Washington cherry, pear, and apple trees from Chatham. It is likely that orchard trees dominated the trees species grown at Chatham during its early years, up until the Civil War. Chatham’s vegetation was transformed by the war, as all vegetation...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Basil Biggs Farm Orchard (1992), Gettysburg National Military Park
note: Due to its location near the center of the Union battle line and its proximity to Union headquarters, the Fisher Farm complex was subjected to intense artillery fire, troop movement, encampment, bivouac, and hospital use. The Fisher Orchard provided cover and an avenue of approach for the Union to and from the main battle line. The orchard is documented on the 1872 topographical survey map and in photographs from...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Leister Orchard, Gettysburg National Military Park
note: The Leister House served as the headquarters for Union General George Meade on July 2 and 3 and was used as a hospital after fighting ceased on July 3. The southern boundary of Fields 2–4 is presently marked by a stone and rider fence. Additionally, the eastern boundary of Field 4 is delineated by a stone and rider fence. Leister Orchard appears to have been planted with both cherries and apples and made up of sli...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Brian Orchard, Gettysburg National Military Park
note: Many orchards dotted the 1863 landscape. Tree fruits provided a valuable food source for subsistence farmers and for livestock. During the Civil War battle, orchards provided cover and concealment, impacted observation, were used as avenues of approach, and became obstacles. This mature apple orchard provided concealment to Union forces stationed in or moving around the northern terminus of Cemetery Ridge. For the...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: William Bliss Farm Orchard (pre-1863), Gettysburg National Military Park
note: Many orchards and fields dotted the 1863 landscape. The farm felds associated with the William Bliss farm became the scenes for some of the most intense and prolonged individual combat of the 1863 Battle of Gettysburg. The Bliss Farm Orchard provided cover and observation for Confederates forces and is documented in photographs from the Tipton Collection and the park’s historical collection.
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Bliss Orchard, Gettysburg National Military Park
note: In the years before the Civil War, typical farms in the Gettysburg area included a homestead, barns, outbuildings, and fields. In the 19th century, farmers began to plant small orchards consisting mostly of apple trees, but some groves contained peaches, cherry, pear, plum, and quince trees. During the Battle of Gettysburg, the Bliss House and Barn was used as fortification and by sharpshooters. The Bliss Farm Orc...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: McPherson Fruit Garden, Gettysburg National Military Park
note: This landscape covers part of the battlefield that witnessed the opening engagement of the Battle of Gettysburg, July 1–3, 1863. It reflects periods of development, as the rural landscape was changing due to shifts in agriculture, urban growth, and battlefield preservation and commemoration. Features like the three major roads, the cuts in an unfinished railroad, series of ridges, patterns of woods, orchards, fenc...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Remnant Apple Orchard, Johnstown Flood National Memorial
note: The landscape commemorates the Johnstown Flood of May 31, 1889. The South Fork Fishing and Hunting Club developed Lake Conemaugh as a private resort for Pittsburgh’s elite in the 1880s. On a hillside meadow overlooking the dam and lakebed is the Lake View Farm, the former farmstead and rehabilitated home of Elias Unger, the club’s president at the time of the disaster. To the southeast of the spring house at the U...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Fruit Trees, Manassas National Battlefield Park
note: Orchards are recorded at both the Henry and Robinson Farms during the period of significance (1724-1962). In 1880, the Henry Farm was planted with 40 apple trees and 100 peach trees, in addition to other agricultural products. Damage to the landscape from the Civil War was extensive. Remnant fruit trees within the study area may date to the period of significance, or may be descended from the original trees. Peach...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Apple Tree, National Capital Parks-East
note: Harmony Hall is significant for its role in exploration and settlement along the Potomac River in Prince George’s County. Battersea, the historic name of the patent on which Harmony Hall is located, was a 500-acre land tract patented in 1662 and was the first patent issued in the area. The landscape is significant for its associations with the agricultural history of the area, Charles Wallace Collins, its architec...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Remnant Orchard, National Capital Parks-East
note: Oxon Hill Farm is associated with the agricultural history of Prince George’s County, Maryland during the 1700s and 1800s. It is also associated with St. Elizabeths Hospital, originally known as the Government Hospital for the Insane. Founded in 1852, St. Elizabeths was the nation’s first federally operated mental health facility. The hospital acquired the farm in 1891 while under the leadership of Doctor William...
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Remnant Orchard, Shenandoah National Park
note: This area of the Blue Ridge Mountains was known and used by people for many centuries, with the first recorded European exploration of the area in 1669. By 1800, many of the local Native Americans had died of disease introduced by European settlers or had moved from the area. Prior to the development of Shenandoah National Park, authorized in 1926, settlers had moved into the mountain hollows where they hunted, fa...

name: Apple Trees, Shenandoah National Park
note: Piney River is a maintenance and trail crew facility in Shenandoah National Park. It is the location of a former CCC camp that was important to the development of Shenandoah National Park and the Skyline Drive, and it still retains CCC buildings. The features of the historic district are associated with the CCC's use of the site. There are several apple trees that likely date to the CCC or early park period.
accessNote: NPS cultural landscape record. Do not take fruit or cuttings without park permission.

name: Pear (specimen), Orchard Remnant, Antietam National Battlefield
note: At the onset of the Civil War, three families owned property near the Burnside Bridge, including the Otto family.
